FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, ABCD 2.0.1 release, 21 research sites across the US, children aged 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis comparing video gamers vs non-video gamers on cognitive task performance and fMRI BOLD signal using ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The gaming exposure variable is explicitly multi-platform: 'Play video games on a computer, console, smart phone, or other devices' — no separate phone-only gaming coefficient exists anywhere in the paper or supplement. Step 2: The composite bundles console, PC, smartphone, and other devices together without a per-device breakout. Per binding clarification, gaming with platform unspecified (or multi-platform aggregated without phone-only isolation) fails FT-IC2.

notes: Paper examines video gaming (multi-platform: computer, console, smartphone, other devices) and cognitive/neural outcomes. No phone-specific or SM-specific variable with its own coefficient exists. Gaming alone without explicit mobile/phone modality fails IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, ABCD 3.0 data release, 21 research sites from 17 states, participants aged 11-14 at time of COVID surveys.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ML analysis (XGBoost/GBT with SHAP) of ABCD longitudinal data predicting pandemic mental health outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Multiple phone/SM-specific variables appear as features with their own SHAP values predicting individual-trait outcomes. Figure 4B shows 'texting on a cell phone, tablet, computer' with SHAP value phi=0.07 predicting depressive symptoms. Figure 1 includes 'time spent browsing the internet' (phi=0.04) and screen time domain features. Figure 3 (anxiety model) includes 'time spent browsing the internet' (phi=0.10). Social media time is listed as a screen time domain feature predicting positive affect and anxiety. These phone/SM-specific features are paired with individual-trait outcomes (depressive symptoms, anxiety, stress, positive affect).

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative SHAP values reported for screen time and texting variables (e.g., 'texting on a cell phone, tablet, computer' phi=0.07 for depressive symptoms; 'time spent browsing the internet' phi=0.10 for anxiety). AUC model performance metrics also reported (mean AUC >= 0.70).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential ML models with SHAP feature importance and AUC metrics reported for SM/phone-individual-trait relationships.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal ABCD cohort with cross-sectional COVID survey assessments; the paper explicitly states 'outcomes that were assessed cross-sectionally.' SM/phone variables appear as IVs (predictors) of mental health outcomes (depressive symptoms, anxiety, stress, positive affect), not as DVs. EC7 does not apply.
notes: ML classification study predicting pandemic mental health. Screen time variables including social media time and texting on cell phone appear as SHAP-ranked predictors (IVs) of anxiety, depression, stress, and positive affect. Cross-sectional outcome assessment confirmed by authors.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study wave 1 release, n=6,758, 21 sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical latent profile analysis (LPA) of fMRI BOLD data from ABCD Study with cross-validated split-half replication.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Biological Psychiatry: Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable appears as IV or DV with its own coefficient in any analysis. The primary analysis identifies neurodevelopmental profiles from fMRI BOLD; screen time appears only as an exploratory group-comparison correlate in Supplemental Table S3 (F=6.80, p<.0001 across profiles) without any inferential SM/phone-to-individual-trait coefficient. Step 2: The screen time variable is described in supplement methods as a 'composite total screen time score' summing all ABCD screen time survey items (TV, gaming, social networking, video chatting, texting) — a multi-activity composite that includes TV and gaming without phone/SM-only breakout. Furthermore, even if the composite qualified, screen time is used only as a clinical correlate of profile membership, not as IV or DV in an inferential SM-individual-trait analysis with its own coefficient.

notes: Primary study identifies latent neurodevelopmental fMRI profiles. Screen time appears only as a demographic/clinical correlate compared across profiles in supplementary table; it is not an IV or DV in an inferential analysis. Screen time composite includes TV, gaming, and other non-phone/SM activities. Fails IC2 on both steps.
